speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] Ruth, you mentioned that things started to change in 1936 with your classmates at school.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember how those changes developed as the years went by?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were more drawn to themselves, you know? They didn't like-- they mixed with us, but sort of your could feel an inner protest. And that mostly happened after school holidays when they went to Germany. I suppose they were indoctrinated. So that where it started.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened on an official level in Poland as the years went by? We're talking now '36, '37, '38. Did anything change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it-- it slowly changed. The Poles were always anti-Semites. That is-- they're famous for it. And I remember, even before the war, they started-- they-- they had people marching before our shop. Don't shop there. That is a Jewish-- that are Jewish premises. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these people? Were they German people or Polish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish. Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was all polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that sort. But I must say, anti-Semitism was always there. That it's not that Hitler brought it in. It is they suck it in with their mother's milk. That is what I say.

speaker: interviewee
text: And talking about it, I was a child of three or four playing in the sand. And I-- I don't know how they smelt it. Maybe I was better dressed than they. I wouldn't know. But I got, you know, sand thrown into my eyes-- I never forget it-- out of hatred.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a play--

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in a park. You know, where sand playgrounds for children. And there I was. And it-- it sat so deeply in me that even till today, I can't forget it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was your first feeling--

speaker: interviewee
text: Experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and and a very deep impression on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. Because I felt I am different.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with the coming of the war? Let's move on now to 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. With the coming of the war, that went practically overnight. My-- well, we all didn't expect that we would be overrun within a fortnight. And as I lived in a border town, my dad said, I take the kids inside to a resort-- Busko, it was called-- that was inside Poland. And they took us there. And paid for our upkeep. And I remember, he had some gold treasures, English pounds. And he gave them to me. They were so heavy, I'll never forget it. I got my first blisters from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was gold gold?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was English one pound things. You know, they look-- they look like our koalas here. The koala gold things we have. And [INAUDIBLE]. And I was hiding that at the top of the wardrobe, you know. And was very frightened about it, because my dad had to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he still had the business. And I was left with my, at that time, 14-year-old brother. And boys are very lively in that age. And it was quite a responsibility for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But within-- four-- within, say, weeks, the war-- not only did the war broke out, but the Germans came in such a speed and overran the little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] township we lived in. And I was sort of cut off from my parents. And I didn't know if I ever will see them. But-- so it went on for a fortnight. And I had good luck, because one day my dad came. And I was so overwhelmed. And I was so proud, because what he gave me to look after, I had it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And gave it nicely to him. And he took us in back. My mom waited in a township closer to home. And first time in my life when I arrived there, because he took us in a horse-drawn peasant thing. And I was dusty because we went one direction in the street, and the German cars [INAUDIBLE], you know, on the way back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was the first time you had seen the German army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, I had seen the German army when they came into this little township we were. But I was-- you know, I was astonished. It didn't take it-- went cars and military equipment. And it-- it was a terrible feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were in the middle of the war. So you parents came-- your father came and got you, and you met up with your mother. Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back home. Back to Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in that few-week period, had Katowice changed? Had anything happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: As if it would have been, you know, hit by [INAUDIBLE], an earthquake or so on. Because we came, and it wasn't-- it was already very hard, us Jewish people, to go back. So we came into a-- the inside of a garbage truck. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were hidden in this truck to come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. Otherwise, you know, they at once had the borders and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anyway, my-- I don't know how my father did it, but he found the driver of the garbage truck who took us back. And I'll never forget, because we came the way where our synagogue stood, and we saw it completely destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that were my first impression. And then-- Which synagogue was this, Ruth?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had only one big synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Katowice?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Katowice. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And immediately, the first thing you saw was the synagogue had been destroyed.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Completely destroyed. Still the bricks were lying around. It-- it was, you know, devastating. And then when we drove to our place, we all got the second shock. In-- in our business in the window was a big picture of Hitler. And underneath was [GERMAN]. 'We thank our Fuhrer.' So that was a very deep shock also for my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your parents' business from that point on?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- it was-- it was given away. What's it call [GERMAN]. But it wasn't a [GERMAN]. It was just the fellow worked for himself, got everything, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a Polish man? Or a German man?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was German. He lived in Katowice as well. Actually, even before the war, wanted to buy the business of my parents. But my parents didn't have the intention to sell it. So when Hitler came, he took it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we just-- our place still was intact. And we just lived from day to day with always new surprises.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the surprises?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that-- I think we got these Jewish band on where you had someone do it. And in the middle was 'Jude' on. That's a-- that's the German's recognizance at once. And was also very hard in food, because it was-- suddenly, everything was cut off. And it didn't take long. There was the first-- the transport for men. It was-- they called it arbeits transport, but what it really was, it was a-- to devastate and tear the family apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my dad was 50 years of age when he had to go to this transport. And they were told they're going for-- to work. But what they really did, they took them to the Russian border. To the Polish-Russian border, actually. And they told them, you go towards Russia. Don't you dare to come back, or you're all shot. So that was the way my father went, with all the others. And we had once notification from him. A letter came through from Lwow. That was Lemberg. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, we never ever heard something from him back. And I-- after the war, we-- I waited. I even had a room for him reserved. I thought he'd comes back. But he never came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so once your father was called for this supposed arbeits thing, you never saw him again.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And I was 19 years of age that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask you, did your parents, in the time leading up to 1939, think about leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They-- you know, they were so well-off that they never thought about any migration. Because as I mentioned before, I had the opportunity-- I was 18 years of age. On my 18th birthday, my then boyfriend asked my father whether he could migrate with me to New Zealand. He had a-- a job offer. He was an optician. My father said, that's the end of the world. I don't let my girl go. So he-- they never ever had the idea of migration.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they-- do you remember your parents and you discussing the possibility of Hitler coming into Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was actually foreseeable, because I remember there came lots of Czechoslovakian Jews whom they also put on the Polish border and sort of sent over. And we, I remember, we adopted a family-- husband, wife-- and even brought their little dog with them. They couldn't part with him. And-- and this fellow said, if somebody can, they should migrate. And at that time, you know, we-- maybe it was the prosperity who didn't give us the idea to migrate. Also, my dad never would have thought that the Germans could do what they did, because he was fighting in World War I with them. So he couldn't imagine that the thing-- you know, that he would be treated as he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once your father disappeared and was taken away, and you, you know, never saw him again, what happened to you? It was you, your mother, your aunts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was a brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: The brother, within six weeks, they did the same to him. Another-- you know, first went the people to 50 years. And then went the young people. And the very old people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went on the same transport, like my father. Same [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] conditions. They sent them to-- wanted them to go to Russia. Only my brother was younger and was in-- in-- I think in the Polish schools they were taught to-- to hate the Russians. Anyway, he came back, and he went to a township, Lublin. There he settled with a Jewish family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he escaped?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He went-- he didn't do what the Germans told him to do, only he went back to Poland. It was occupied Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now there were four-- four women left? There was your mother, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, four women. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your aunts?

speaker: interviewee
text: One aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: One aunt. Three women.

speaker: interviewee
text: One aunt, in the meantime, passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So where were you living at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still in Katowice, in our place.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your flat?

speaker: interviewee
text: IN-- in our flat. Only they came, the Germans, and we had a big place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the lunch and dining room sort of-- they sealed off from the other rooms. And they said not to go in. And sort of we lived-- they put seals on-- on the locks. And we lived in our bedrooms and our kitchen for the time being.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in-- in the-- the part of your flat?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed till, I think, '40. It was a good few months after my brother left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this would have been, what, the beginning of 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: '40. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we were transferred to Chrzanow, 'shanuf' in Polish. That was sort of an open ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where was Chrzanow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chrzanow was between Katowice and Krakow. That was just halfway. It was a little township also. Very Jewish township. And we were very friendly greeted by the Jews. They were Polish Jews. But they didn't differentiate at all that we actually came as German-speaking people. They were exceptional good and shared what they had with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you-- you mentioned that this was an open ghetto. What is an open ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: An open ghetto is a ghetto with a few street where Jews could live and work. But we were not wired in, like, say, a real ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no wall?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no wall, and no wire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you move out of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we shouldn't. We shouldn't move out.

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't meant to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not meant to. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did only Jews live in this area, this ghetto area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. There were Poles as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a mixture, in this ghetto area, of Jews and Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember especially, the house or flat we lived in, there were, on the other side, were Poles. And that was-- opposite was our flat. And thanks to the Poles, when we were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] settle-- resettled-- not resettled, but taken away, they actually told the Germans where we were hiding. We had a hiding place, but they sort of said, there are Jews. And when we had to come out and so on, my mother wanted to bribe this German fellow. And he said, look, I gladly would. But the Poles said it to my commander, so I can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were ultimately betrayed or-- by your neighbors? By your Polish neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: By-- yes, by the Poles. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Chrzanow, you were given a flat or a room? Where did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My-- we-- we had a room and a kitchen. That was all we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do? Did-- were you given any kind of work? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked. I worked in a factory where military coats were sewn. And my mom was secretary to a very high German Nazi. Because she was very good in stenography and typing. And in the German language, as such, so that she had this position.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- you-- how long did you live in the open ghetto in Chrzanow?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were the last transport, because they took us away in transports. But we were the last. And it was the 18th February, '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remained in Chrzanow through '40 and '41--

speaker: interviewee
text: '41 and '42, and the beginning of '43, we were liquidated. The ghetto was liquidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about conditions in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, conditions, food-wise, which is the most important part, was not very easy to survive. My mom had special ability to earn still a little bit, and through our German house [GERMAN]--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's a house [GERMAN]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a-- it was a-- like here, a manager. A manager for properties.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- he was our manager for-- my parents' manager for 20 years, because our-- some of our properties were in Breslau. And we were-- Katowice was Poland, and Breslau was Germany. So we had this fellow who looked after everything. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gustaf Wagner.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had been employed by your parents for-- for two decades before?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. He came twice yearly to us and brought us whatever he collected and discussed things. And he was actually, by profession, a policeman that my father sort of taught in the art of managing things, and so on, that he ended up to have owned his own four properties. Because he-- he was a good pupil, I suppose.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother was in contact with this Gustaf Wagner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He-- he brought her money. And they were good to us. Even food, which helped a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I personally found out after the war that he was an SS man. But to me-- because I suppose my parents were so outstanding to him and made something out of him-- he was exceptional decent. When I was in the concentration camp, and through sort of the back doors, I was a painter, and I said, would you take a letter for me? So he took the letter, and I had contact with this Wagner.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was so nice that he brought to the painter food for me. And the painter brought it in in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- you know, at that time, I didn't know who he is. And so I didn't know what influence he had, because he also, while I was in the camp, through him, he made contact. He found out where my brother is. How my brother is. So actually, I couldn't fault him. So this Gustaf Wagner, who-- who was German and who you subsequently found out was in the SS, for you--

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- after the war, I found that out, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: To you he was-- he behaved really well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. To-- to that that extent, when I escaped from the camp and I came to him, then today I understand why he couldn't keep me. It would have been-- for him it would have been a question of life and death when I would have been discovered.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're jumping ahead a little bit--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with all of this, but-- but he's an important part.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an important part of me being alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What went on in the ghetto? What's-- how did the Germans treat the Jews in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very badly, because they had to survive. So some sold different foodstuff, and so on. You know, not in shops, but, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] say, from the Poles they bought a calf and they killed it and so on, and they had to distribute it or sell it. And when they were caught, that was life sentence. And what was awful is they hang-- I never forget that-- they hang a fellow. And they called us all out to see the hanging that nobody, most likely, would dare to do the wrong step.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they made an-- they made an example of him, and you got to watch?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's exactly right. And so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it-- it-- also, all of us were working. Slave labor. But we could come home, which was better than when we were then in the real camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was the ghetto-- who organized the ghetto? Was there a Jewish organization within the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that is right. They picked a-- a sort of-- you know, they organized the people. What their dirty work, Jewish people did. Like calling us when the transports were-- like-- like, even by my father. The Germans didn't do it. There was a Jewish congregation, or whatever you want to call it, and they called everybody there. And they organized it, and then they handed it over to the Germans. And same was in the ghetto. Jewish people were picked to organize all bad things for us. That exactly was the case. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there were-- in the ghetto were several transport. What's it called? To arbeitslagers. And we were lucky enough to go with last one. I suppose my mom bought us out, sort of, that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you think that the reason that you remained in the open ghetto for so long was that your mother managed that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's exactly right. She was a fantastic organizer. And I inherited it. Therefore, I'm alive. You know, when she sold different things-- so she had one German policeman who carried it for her. So she never could be attacked, because she never had anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she actually organized your survival in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: She organized my survival. Exactly.
